name: Torrecampo and Conquista Loop from Villanueva de Córdoba
name_original: Conquista y Torrecampo — circular desde Villanueva de Córdoba
description: This difficult 32.6-mile touring cycling route from Villanueva de Córdoba explores the scenic dehesa landscape of the Pedroches region.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Villanueva de Córdoba is characterized by its expansive dehesa landscape, featuring extensive holm oak groves and cork oaks. The region lies within the undulating plains of Los Pedroches, ranging between 600 and 700 meters above sea level, offering varied terrain. To the south, the Sierra Morena mountain range provides routes with significant elevation gains. Numerous streams and reservoirs, such as the Guadalmellato Reservoir, also define the area's natural features. Best touring cycling routes around Villanueva De Córdoba The most popular touring cycling route is Torrecampo and Conquista Loop from Villanueva de Córdoba, a difficult 32.6 miles (52.4 km) trail that takes 3 hours 14 minutes to complete. This route traverses the dehesa landscape with varied gradients. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Conquista, Azuel and Cardeña — circular from Villanueva de Córdoba, a difficult 46.0 miles (74.1 km) path. This route explores the Sierra Morena foothills, offering challenging climbs and scenic views. Local touring cyclists also love the Los Pedroches Loop, a 41.4 miles (66.6 km) trail leading through the undulating plains and holm oak groves of the dehesa landscape, often completed in about 4 hours 23 minutes. Touring cycling around Villanueva de Córdoba is defined by the expansive dehesa landscape, the challenging terrain of the Sierra Morena, and the undulating plains of Los Pedroches. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes. The routes in Villanueva de Córdoba are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. More than 50 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Villanueva de Córdoba's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Villanueva de Córdoba?
A: There are 10 dedicated touring cycling routes around Villanueva de Córdoba. These routes cater to various fitness levels, with 2 easy, 3 moderate, and 5 difficult options available.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes around Villanueva de Córdoba?
A: The terrain is quite varied. You'll primarily cycle through the expansive &lt;em&gt;dehesa landscape&lt;/em&gt;, characterized by undulating plains between 600 and 700 meters above sea level, dotted with holm and cork oaks. Towards the south, the Sierra Morena mountain range offers more challenging routes with significant elevation gains and quiet roads through Mediterranean forests.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes around Villanueva de Córdoba are circular. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1362957633/torrecampo-and-conquista-loop-from-villanueva-de-cordoba" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torrecampo and Conquista Loop from Villanueva de Córdoba&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1362979782/conquista-azuel-and-cardena-circular-from-villanueva-de-cordoba" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Conquista, Azuel and Cardeña — circular from Villanueva de Córdoba&lt;/a&gt; are popular circular options that allow you to start and finish in the same location.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks I can see while cycling?
A: You'll encounter the unique &lt;em&gt;dehesa landscape&lt;/em&gt; with its ancient holm oak groves, part of Europe's largest. The region also features numerous streams and reservoirs, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/929932" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalmellato Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. The Sierra Morena provides stunning mountain views, and the area is recognized as a Starlight Reserve, offering exceptional stargazing opportunities on clear nights.

Q: Are there any routes suitable for families or less experienced cyclists?
A: Yes, there are 2 easy touring cycling routes available. While specific family-friendly routes are not detailed, these easier options typically feature less elevation gain and shorter distances, making them more accessible for beginners or those cycling with children. The undulating plains of the dehesa can offer gentler gradients.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Villanueva de Córdoba?
A: The best time for touring cycling in Villanueva de Córdoba is generally during spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, temperatures are milder, and the landscapes are vibrant. Summers can be very hot, making cycling less comfortable, while winters are generally mild but can have cooler, wetter days.

Q: Where can I find parking when planning a cycling trip?
A: Villanueva de Córdoba, as a town, offers various public parking options. For routes starting directly from the town, you can typically find street parking or designated parking areas. For routes venturing into natural parks or more remote areas, it's advisable to check specific starting points for available parking facilities, which are often found near trailheads or small villages.

Q: Are there any challenging routes for experienced touring cyclists?
A: Absolutely. The region offers 5 difficult touring cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38158812" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Virgen de Luna – Conquista loop from Villanueva de Córdoba&lt;/a&gt; covers over 143 km with significant elevation gain, exploring the Sierra Morena foothills. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38158836" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Virgen de Luna – Villanueva to Cardeña Trail loop from Villanueva de Córdoba-Los Pedroches&lt;/a&gt;, which is over 122 km long.

Q: What do other cyclists say about the touring routes in Villanueva de Córdoba?
A: The touring cycling routes in Villanueva de Córdoba are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the unique dehesa landscape, and the challenging climbs offered by the Sierra Morena.

Q: Are there opportunities for stargazing along any cycling routes?
A: Yes, the area around Villanueva de Córdoba, including the Dehesa de la Jara, is recognized as a Starlight Reserve. While not a specific cycling route for stargazing, routes that traverse this area, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1363007529/los-pedroches-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Pedroches Loop&lt;/a&gt;, would offer excellent opportunities for stargazing if you plan an evening ride or an overnight bikepacking trip.

Q: Can I find places to eat or drink along the cycling routes?
A: While specific cafes or pubs directly on every route are not listed, many routes connect or pass through small towns and villages like Torrecampo, Conquista, Azuel, and Cardeña. These settlements typically offer local establishments where you can refuel and refresh during your ride. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer or more remote sections.

Q: What is the significance of the Dehesa landscape for touring cyclists?
A: The Dehesa landscape, often called the 'Mar de Encinas' (sea of holm oaks), is Europe's largest dehesa forest. For touring cyclists, it offers a unique and picturesque setting with extensive holm and cork oak groves. The undulating plains provide varied terrain, from gentle slopes to more challenging ascents, all within a culturally rich and biodiverse ecosystem that supports wildlife and traditional livestock like Iberian pigs.

Without a doubt, a good place to refuel.

In the central square of the town of Villanueva de Córdoba are the anti-aircraft shelter from the Civil War (it is the best preserved in the province) and the Church of San Miguel Arcángel (from the 16th century).

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this touring cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult for touring cycling. While some sections may be wide tracks (pistas anchas), expect challenging unpaved segments and potentially rougher surfaces that require good fitness and cycling skills. It is not recommended for beginners.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for completing this touring cycling route is approximately 3 hours and 14 minutes. This can vary based on your fitness level, the type of bike you're using, and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied, featuring the picturesque 'dehesa' (wooded pastureland) characteristic of the region. You'll encounter a mix of 'pistas anchas' (wide tracks) and more demanding unpaved sections. Be prepared for rougher surfaces, as some parts of the route may not be consistently smooth or easily accessible.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The dehesa landscape is particularly beautiful after periods of rain, when the fields are vibrant green and small streams flow, creating a scene more typical of spring. Therefore, late winter to spring (February to May) would likely offer the most scenic conditions. Avoid the peak summer months due to high temperatures.

faqs.question: Are there any notable sights or landmarks along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through or near several interesting points. You'll cycle through the settlement of Conquista and near the Evangelical Church Philadelphia in Torrecampo. The starting point in Villanueva de Córdoba is also home to the Plaza de España.

faqs.question: Is this touring cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While there are no specific restrictions against dogs on this route, it's important to consider the challenging terrain and distance (over 52 km). If you bring your dog, ensure they are fit enough for a long, difficult ride, and always keep them on a leash, especially when passing through settlements or near livestock.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead in Villanueva de Córdoba?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Villanueva de Córdoba. You should be able to find parking within the town, particularly near the Plaza de España or other public parking areas, to begin your circular tour.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this circular route. It traverses public lands and tracks, so you are free to enjoy it without additional costs.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with sections of other trails. You will find it overlaps with parts of the Transandalus, Camino de Villanueva de Córdoba a Fuencaliente, Carril de la Cañada de Santa María, Camino de la Lagartojas, Camino Real de Villanueva de la Mancha, Cordel de Torrecampo, and Camino de Villanueva de Córdoba a los Molinos de la Rivera de Casillas.

faqs.question: Are there any sections of the route that might be closed or difficult to navigate?
faqs.answer: Be aware that some accounts from cyclists in the area have mentioned encountering 'caminos cerrados al público' (paths closed to the public) or challenging sections requiring significant effort. While this route is mapped, it's advisable to be prepared for potential detours or rougher conditions in certain unpaved areas.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a touring cycling trip on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, you should bring plenty of water, snacks, a repair kit for your bike (including spare tubes), a first-aid kit, and appropriate clothing for the weather. A GPS device or a downloaded map is highly recommended, as some sections might be less clearly marked.
